world_map.raw:
Center point: 50.7753° N, 6.0839° E (Aachen)
Reach radius/trade zone description: Carolingian coinage spread across France, Germany, Northern Italy, and Northeast Spain; the franc à cheval's story later connects to Poitiers and London (Jean II's captivity)
Key nodes: Aachen (50.7753° N, 6.0839° E); Corvey Abbey (51.7639° N, 9.4083° E); Poitiers (46.5802° N, 0.3404° E); Paris (48.8566° N, 2.3522° E); Melle mint, Poitou-Charentes (46.2231° N, 0.1428° W)
Suggested map era: 793–1364 AD
Historical map source: BnF Gallica digitized coin collection; Centre Charlemagne museum exhibits on Aachen's palace complex

quiz.q: Who introduced the pound-shilling-pence monetary reform formalized in 794 AD?
Who was Fastrada, and why does her coin matter?
quiz.a: Charlemagne's third wife — the first Carolingian coin to name a queen

quiz.q: Why was the franc à cheval first minted in 1360?
quiz.a: To raise the ransom money to free King Jean II, captured at the Battle of Poitiers

quiz.q: Why was the franc à cheval abandoned by 1364?
quiz.a: Its gold content cost more than its face value

quiz.q: What does "franc" possibly refer to in the coin's name?
quiz.a: Either "Francorum Rex" (King of the Franks) or the king being made "free" (franc) after ransom payment

decision_simulation:
Scenario: It's 1360. France must raise 3 million gold écus to free King Jean II from English captivity. The crown must decide how to fund this urgently.
Options: (1) Mint a new high-value gold coin (the franc à cheval) specifically for the ransom; (2) Raise taxes sharply across the kingdom; (3) Negotiate a reduced ransom or delay payment
Historical outcome: France minted the franc à cheval to help raise the ransom — but the coin's gold cost proved too expensive to sustain, and it was discontinued within four years, even though the name "franc" endured for centuries afterward
EXPLORE IT (CURRENTLY OPEN — highest priority)

understand.economic_impact:
One-line summary: Isabelle represents the moment France's money system was built directly out of a national crisis — a king's ransom — and that crisis-born currency lasted for centuries.
Three key economic shifts: (1) Charlemagne's 794 reform created a standardized pound-shilling-pence system that structured European money for a millennium; (2) the franc à cheval showed the danger of tying a coin's value too closely to its metal cost — it collapsed within four years; (3) the name "franc" outlived the specific coin, becoming the enduring name of French currency for over 600 years
Modern parallel: emergency government bonds or special-purpose currencies issued during a national crisis — created to solve one urgent problem, sometimes outliving their original purpose
understand.political_impact:
One-line summary: The franc's birth from a king's ransom is a permanent reminder that a nation's money and its national pride are deeply tied together.
Three key political shifts: (1) Charlemagne's currency reform was a tool for unifying and projecting power across his empire; (2) Fastrada's coin broke convention by naming a queen, a small political shift in how royal power was displayed; (3) the franc à cheval turned a national humiliation (the king's capture) into a symbol of chivalric resolve, showing how currency can be political propaganda
Modern parallel: nations today still design currency imagery to project strength or unity, especially after moments of crisis or transition (like the euro's design after the franc was retired)
understand.why_it_still_matters:
Plain-language answer: France's most famous historical currency, the franc, was literally created to pay off a king's ransom after he was captured in war. Money isn't just abstract numbers — it's often born out of very real, very human emergencies.
Connection to today's world: The name "franc" survived for over 600 years after that one emergency coin was minted, showing how something created to solve a short-term crisis can become a permanent part of a country's identity — until France gave it up for the euro in 1999, a loss Isabelle still feels personally.


CHARACTER #5 — LARS VAN HOLST
Dutch Guilder (NLG) — Origin: 1378
Tagline: "He financed the first stock exchange the world ever built, and he'd do it again tonight."
1. WATCH IT — Documentary
Core narrative arc: Open on the 1378 gold guilder of Willem V of Bavaria, Count of Holland — a regional coin, not yet a national currency — then leap to 1602: the founding of the VOC, the world's first modern joint-stock company, and the 1608–1611 construction of the Beurs van Hendrick de Keyser, the world's first purpose-built stock exchange. Anchor sequence: the September 9, 1606 VOC share certificate, the oldest surviving share certific
